retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Cephalometric Analysis -- Technique or Treatment -- Standardized Lateral Cephalometric Radiographs
retrieved_docs.content: Lateral skull radiographs provide a 2-dimensional representation of the head and neck and measure the sagittal and vertical dimensions of the skull. Sagittal measurements assess the position and inclination of the maxilla and mandible, while vertical measurements evaluate the height of the facial structures and the relationship between the jaw. [4] In contrast, posteroanterior radiographs, taken from the front of the head, measure transverse and vertical dimensions, offering insights into the width of the face and the relationship between the jaws in the transverse plane. [5] However, in clinical practice, cephalometric analysis primarily relies on lateral radiographs, as posteroanterior projections are more challenging to interpret.

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Cephalometric Analysis -- Clinical Significance -- Anteroposterior Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: ANB angle: The ANB angle evaluates the anteroposterior relationship between the maxilla and mandible. [12] The ANB angle is calculated as the difference between the SNA (sella-nasion to A-point) and SNB (sella-nasion to B-point) angles, using the formula: ANB = SNA - SNB. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Cephalometric Analysis -- Clinical Significance -- Anteroposterior Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: SNB angle: The SNB angle evaluates the anteroposterior position of the mandible to the anterior cranial base. [9] The SNB angle is formed by connecting the sella, nasion, and B-point. The average SNB angle is 78 ± 3 degrees. [10] An increased SNB angle indicates that the mandible is positioned anteriorly relative to the cranial base compared to the average. [11] Conversely, a decreased SNB angle suggests that the mandible is retruded relative to the cranial base compared to the norm. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Cephalometric Analysis -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Cephalometric analysis assesses the anteroposterior and vertical relationships of the mandible and maxilla in relation to the cranial base and each other, as well as the upper and lower teeth in relation to the mandibular and maxillary bones (see Image. Common Cephalometric Points). The analysis involves comparing patient-specific proportions and angle measurements with population averages, which can be obtained through manual tracing or digital methods. The results of the cephalometric analysis are subject to projection errors and measurement variability and are dependent on the operator. Therefore, the cephalometric values for an individual patient must be interpreted in the clinical context, as deviations from average values may be compensated by other features in the face or skull. The tables below describe commonly used cephalometric points (see Table 1 ) and planes (see Table 2 ). [2] [3] Table 2. Cephalometric Planes

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Cephalometric Analysis -- Clinical Significance -- Vertical Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The maxillary-mandibular plane angle (MMPA) assesses the vertical relationship between the maxilla and mandible. The MMPA is formed by projecting lines from the mandibular and maxillary planes until they meet posteriorly. The average MMPA value is 27 ± 4 degrees. A normal MMPA indicates a well-proportioned lower face and a normal overbite. An increased MMPA value suggests a long lower face and an open bite, while a decreased MMPA value indicates a shorter lower face and a closed bite. [15] Incisor Position: Angular Evaluation

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Cephalometric Analysis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The technique of cephalometric analysis has a rich history, originating in the late 1800s when radiographs were first used to study the head and neck. In the 1930s, Holly Broadbent, a professor of orthodontics at the University of Michigan, advanced the field by analyzing the relationship between the teeth and the skull. This pioneering work involved measuring various angles and distances on radiographic images, thereby establishing the foundations of cephalometric analysis. [1] Over the following decades, researchers expanded on this foundational work, developing additional methods such as the Wits analysis. Cephalometric analysis is an integral part of modern orthodontics practice, aiding in the diagnosis and treatment of various dental and skeletal anomalies. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Cephalometric Analysis -- Clinical Significance -- Anteroposterior Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: An increased SNA angle indicates that the maxilla is positioned protrusively relative to the cranial base compared to the average. [3] Conversely, a decreased SNA angle suggests that the maxilla is retruded relative to the cranial base compared to the norm. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Cephalometric Analysis -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Cephalometric analysis helps diagnose dental and skeletal malocclusions, plan corrective treatments, and evaluate treatment outcomes and growth changes.

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Cephalometric Analysis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Cephalometric analysis evaluates lateral skull radiographs obtained with a cephalostat to determine skeletal patterns and assess treatment complexity. This technique is specifically indicated for cases involving planned anteroposterior movements but is not necessary for all orthodontic treatments. The use of cephalometric analysis is particularly justified when significant modifications to the incisor position are anticipated.

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Cephalometric Analysis -- Clinical Significance -- Incisor Position: Linear Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The linear measurement of the maxilla is taken from the maxillary incisor to nasion-A, while the linear measurement of the mandible is taken from the mandibular incisor to nasion-B. These measurements describe the relationship between the tooth and its supporting basal bone, indicating whether the tooth is in a normal, procumbent (ahead of the supporting bone), or recumbent (behind the supporting bone) position. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Cephalometric Analysis -- Clinical Significance -- Anteroposterior Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: SNA angle: The SNA angle assesses the anteroposterior position of the maxilla relative to the anterior cranial base. [9] The SNA angle is formed by connecting the sella, nasion, and A-point. The average SNA angle is 81 ± 3 degrees. For example, a patient with an SNA angle of 82 degrees demonstrates a well-positioned maxilla relative to the cranial base. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Cephalometric Analysis -- Clinical Significance -- Vertical Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The angular measurement of the maxilla is determined by measuring the angle from the incisor to nasion-A, while the angular measurement of the mandible is calculated from the incisor to nasion-B. These values indicate the inclination of teeth—proclined teeth are angled forward, retroclined teeth are tilted backward, and normally inclined teeth are in a neutral position. [8] The normal incisor to nasion-A angle is 22 degrees, and the normal incisor to nasion-B angle is 25 degrees. An increased incisor to nasion-A or nasion-B angle indicates proclination of the incisor, while a decreased angle suggests retroclination. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Cephalometric Analysis -- Clinical Significance -- Interpretation of Cephalometric Analysis Results
retrieved_docs.content: Average angular measurements and proportions are established for the general population, but these values serve as general guidelines. Standard measures may vary based on age, sex, and ethnicity. [8] A comprehensive orthodontic assessment must incorporate the patient’s unique skeletal and dental characteristics alongside cephalometric findings.

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Cephalometric Analysis -- Clinical Significance -- Vertical Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The position of mandibular incisors is further evaluated by the angle formed between the long axis of the tooth and the mandibular plane, which extends from gonion to gnathion. [10] The normal mandibular incisor to mandibular plane angle (Go-Gn) is 87 degrees. An increased mandibular incisor to mandibular plane angle indicates that the incisors are proclined, whereas a decreased value indicates the incisors are retroclined. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Malocclusion -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: One such radiograph unique to orthodontics is the cephalometric projection. The cephalometric projections capture a lateral image of the face whereby relationships between the skull and the jaws can be made. These projections can additionally help orthodontists predict the dental age of a patient and growth potential and highlight sagittal discrepancies, all of which can aid treatment planning. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Cephalometric Analysis -- Clinical Significance -- Anteroposterior Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The average ANB angle for a class I skeletal pattern is 2 degrees. An ANB angle greater than 4 degrees suggests a class II skeletal pattern, while an angle less than 2 degrees indicates a class III skeletal pattern. [13] However, the ANB angle can vary based on the position of the nasion and the prominence of the lower face. When the ANB angle is abnormally increased or decreased, alternative methods such as the Wits analysis should be considered.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2813
retrieved_docs.content: Ultrasonography of head and neck is classified as a form of diagnostic ultrasound of the head and neck.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2341
retrieved_docs.content: The mentum presentation of the fetus, in which the fetal chin is the leading part during delivery, is classified as a مّجيء وَجهي (face presentation). Recognizing this presentation is important for obstetric assessment and delivery planning, as it influences the choice of birth method and the management of potential complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2107
retrieved_docs.content: Skull and face fractures, described in Arabic as كسر في قبو القحف, refer to injuries involving breaks in the cranial vault and the facial bones, encompassing a range of traumatic damage to the head and facial structures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 256
retrieved_docs.content: diverse injury types (intracranial, skeletal, facial), their specific anatomical subcategories, corresponding treatment modalities, and long‑term sequelae are interrelated, while also incorporating psychosocial determinants such as aggression that may influence injury occurrence and outcome. This integrated framework supports comprehensive assessment, classification, and management of patients presenting with complex traumatic or toxic presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 225
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a direct procedure performed on zuby (teeth), indicating that the teeth are the specific anatomical site for this dental intervention. Disorders of teeth and jaw are described as having zuby as their parent category, meaning that conditions affecting the teeth and jaw are organized under the broader classification of zuby. Consequently, zuby functions both as the procedural target for single‑tooth extraction and as the foundational element within the hierarchy of dental and jaw disorders, underscoring its central importance in dental treatment and pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 277
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures on Nose are associated with nasal disorder (اِضطِراب أنفي).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 57
retrieved_docs.content: on nose, mouth and pharynx” and to plastic procedures on the nose, reflecting its relevance to head‑and‑neck and cosmetic/reconstructive surgery. Collectively, these procedures demonstrate that Excision functions as a versatile operative strategy adaptable to endocrine, hepatobiliary, neurosurgical, spinal, musculoskeletal, urologic, breast, and craniofacial contexts, supporting comprehensive patient assessment, accurate coding, and optimized multidisciplinary care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is classified as a malignant tumor in the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان), indicating that this metastatic cancer represents a specific form of malignant neoplasm affecting the sublingual salivary gland. Recognizing this relationship clarifies the pathological nature and precise anatomical location of the disease, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning in patients with salivary gland malignancies.

Together, these interrelated entities illustrate that malignant bone tumors of the skull and facial region are integrated within broader head and neck oncology, can arise as primary or metastatic lesions, and are represented across multiple linguistic and classification frameworks, which is essential for comprehensive diagnosis, coding, and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 227

Collectively, these interrelated hierarchies integrate psychological index terms, injury classifications, anatomical sites, specific traumatic diagnoses, fracture subtypes, superficial injuries, late sequelae, violent behaviors, and diagnostic‑therapeutic pathways, providing a comprehensive framework for documentation, assessment, and patient‑centered care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2301
retrieved_docs.content: Head and neck injury is linked to a broader category of intracranial injury (إصابة داخل الجمجمة). Within this category, a fracture of the anterior fossa and a fracture of the middle fossa are each classified as specific types of intracranial injury, highlighting the hierarchical relationship among head and neck trauma, general intracranial damage, and distinct skull‑base fracture subtypes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2192
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of bone of the face is a specific type of neoplasm of bone of the face, representing a malignant tumor that originates in the facial bones. This relationship indicates that the broader category “neoplasm of bone of the face” encompasses the more specific entity “primary malignant neoplasm of bone of the face,” clarifying the hierarchical classification of facial bone tumors for diagnostic and therapeutic considerations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 214
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of bone and connective tissue is further linked to distinct skeletal malignancies, including malignant neoplasm of the frontal bone, occipital bone, parietal bone, temporal bone and the Arabic‑language malignant tumor of the upper jaw (ورم خبيث في الفك العلوي). Collectively, these relationships highlight the interconnected impact of tobacco exposure, antidepressant therapy, and the diverse malignant entities affecting the skull, facial bones, specific cranial bones and associated lymphatic structures, underscoring the need for comprehensive evaluation and coordinated management in patients with cranio‑facial malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1126
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of bone and connective tissue is linked to the broader Arabic concept “ورم العظام” (bone tumor) and is specifically mapped to “ورم خبيث في الفك العلوي” (malignant tumor of the upper jaw). In addition, the Arabic term “سرطان العظم” (bone cancer) is classified as a malignant tumor of the upper jaw. Together, these relationships indicate that cancer of bone and connective tissue is understood as a type of malignant upper‑jaw tumor, connecting the general notion of a bone tumor with a precise anatomical manifestation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1924
retrieved_docs.content: not considered forms of Excision. Excision is also linked to procedures involving the head and neck region. It is identified as the “method_of” other or therapeutic procedures on the nose, mouth, and pharynx, and it has a relationship with plastic procedures on the nose, suggesting that excisional techniques are employed both in therapeutic and aesthetic interventions of the nasal area. Overall, the knowledge base positions Excision as a central operative modality encompassing a wide range of minimally invasive and open surgical techniques across orthopedics, general surgery, neurosurgery, and endocrine surgery, while simultaneously defining its boundaries by excluding specific laminectomy approaches and connecting it to both therapeutic and plastic procedures of the facial region.
